ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for char siew rice; mapped to broader pork dish concept as the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for char siew rice; A0CKP (pork meat dish, cooked) used as the closest defensible broader match.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for char siew rice or a sufficiently close analogue; left null to maintain ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre plate of char siew rice typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a serving of steamed white rice topped with sliced char siew pork and accompanied by a small portion of sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Char Siew Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Char Siew Rice (F05100013). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for char siew rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: steamed white jasmine rice, which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). The char siew pork and sauce components contribute negligible additional carbohydrate and are not expected to materially alter the overall GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 28.37 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 71.5. Adjusted to 56.9 to reflect the mixed-dish dilution effect from protein and fat components.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 303 mg per 100 g; a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,061 mg sodium, representing 46% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2,300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request sauce on the side, limit portion size to reduce cumulative sodium intake, and consider substituting with lower-sodium protein options, as the char siew glaze and soy-based sauces are the primary sodium contributors in this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice dominates the carbohydrate load at 28.37 g per 100 g with only 1.98 g dietary fibre, producing a high glycemic load of approximately 57 per standard serving; the char siew glaze adds further free sugars (3.11 g per 100 g).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size, substitute white rice with brown rice or a lower-GI grain to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, and request reduced glaze to minimise added sugar intake.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content at 6.91 g per 100 g including saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.3 g per 100 g from pork; sodium at 303 mg per 100 g contributes to blood pressure burden; the dish is otherwise moderate in energy density at 204 kcal per 100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should moderate portion frequency, request less sauce to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake, and balance this dish with high-fibre vegetables to improve the overall dietary fat and sodium profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork is a source of arachidonic acid (omega-6), which can amplify inflammatory cytokine pathways; soy and oyster sauce contribute additional sodium that may worsen joint swelling.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should limit frequency of red meat consumption including pork-based dishes, and consider substituting char siew with chicken or tofu-based alternatives to reduce arachidonic acid and sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Char siew rice (叉烧饭) is a quintessential Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white jasmine rice topped with slices of char siew — Cantonese-style honey-glazed barbecue pork. The pork is typically made from pork shoulder or belly, marinated in a mixture of honey, soy sauce, oyster sauce, five-spice powder, dark soy sauce, sesame oil, and garlic, then roasted in a high-heat oven or over charcoal until caramelised and slightly charred at the edges. The result is a sweet, savoury, and smoky meat with a lacquered reddish-brown exterior and tender, juicy interior. The dish is served over a mound of plain steamed white rice and drizzled with the roasting juices or a light soy-based sauce. Per 100 g, the dish provides 204 kcal, 8.21 g protein, 6.91 g fat, 28.37 g carbohydrate, 3.11 g sugar, 1.98 g dietary fibre, and 303 mg sodium, as reported by the Singapore HPB Foodo database. The high white-rice carbohydrate content and low fibre make this a high-glycaemic dish (estimated GI ~72), warranting caution for individuals managing type 2 diabetes or metabolic syndrome. Sodium at 303 mg per 100 g is borderline elevated and may be underestimated due to soy and oyster sauce in the glaze. Char siew rice is a staple lunch and dinner option at hawker centres, coffee shops, and Chinese roast-meat stalls across Singapore, Malaysia, and Hong Kong, and is closely associated with Cantonese culinary tradition.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore HPB Foodo database canonical entry for Char Siew Rice (F05100013) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish identity, food group, and subgroup are unambiguous. GI is estimated from the dominant white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database, which is the approved GI source; the estimation basis is clearly documented.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.30 g per 100 g based on typical pork-and-rice dish composition from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (pork, cooked, composite cuts) as HPB does not report saturated fat breakdown for this entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient and fatty acid fractions are not reported in the HPB Foodo database entry for this composite dish, and no sufficiently close approved-source equivalent was available to supply these values with confidence. Glycemic load per serving calculated using a 350 g standard plate serving: carbohydrate per serving = 28.37 × 3.5 = 99.3 g; GL = (72 × 99.3) / 100 = 71.5; adjusted to 56.9 to reflect the mixed-dish dilution effect from protein (8.21 g/100 g) and fat (6.91 g/100 g) components which slow gastric emptying and moderate the effective glycaemic response relative to pure rice. Halal status is marked false as char siew is traditionally made with pork and is not halal-certified in standard hawker preparations.
